text: The CT scan shows an adrenal cyst on the right side in a fifty-year-old female patient. The standard port placement for adrenalectomy is visible. The surgeon begins dissection between the liver and right adrenal gland, using a harmonic scalpel to lift the liver for better access to the adrenal cyst. An instrument is applied to hold the liver upwards.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the upper pole of the right kidney and the thin-walled, bluish adrenal cyst, which measures approximately five centimeters. The upper pole of the kidney is visible on the left side of the surgical field as the correct dissection plane is identified.

Small bleeding vessels are coagulated as the dissection progresses towards the superior part of the adrenal cyst. The chromaffin tissue is clearly visible on the cyst's surface. The surgeon carefully identifies the adrenal vein for ligation, while the adrenal artery is not visible.

The adrenal cyst is punctured, causing fluid to escape and the cyst to collapse. A vessel on the inferior aspect of the adrenal gland is identified, respected, and ligated with clips. The lumen of the vessel is visible. The surgeon dissects the adrenal vein and applies a ligar clip to secure it.

The surgeon applies a third clip to the adrenal vein but decides not to cut it due to insufficient space between the second and third clips. The dissection continues, focusing on separating the upper pole of the kidney from the adrenal cyst. Clips are placed cautiously to prevent oozing from small veins. The superior aspect of the adrenal cyst is prepared for final removal.

The surgeon applies clips to control bleeding tissue at the inferior aspect of the liver after removing the adrenal cyst specimen. Hemostasis is performed to address small tears and signature marks visible on the liver surface.
